event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Program:** MAAP-1 "AetherForge" |

**Contracting Entity:** Eurus Systems
**Document Type:** Program

Management Plan Update — Formal Change Action
**Change Reference

:** CCB-MAAP1-SC-001 (Pending CCB Approval)
**

Proposed Change:** Primary Battery Supplier Substitution — Samsung SDI → LG Energy Solution
**Contract Type

:** Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
**Distribution:** Program Manager,
Chief Engineer, Supply Chain Director, Certification Manager, Program

VP, CFO
**Classification:** Program Sensitive — Internal Distribution

Only
**Revision:** Draft for CCB Review

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update documents the program
matic, technical, financial, and supply chain implications of the proposed substitution of LG Energy Solution (
LGES) as the primary battery pack supplier for the MAAP-1 "AetherForge" program, replacing Samsung SDI in
that role. The update is prepared in accordance with the Program Requirements Baseline (PRB) Section
12 change control requirements and provides the Program Control
Board (CCB) with a complete, cross-functional impact picture necessary

to adjudicate this change.

This document supersedes or amends the corresponding sections
of the standing MAAP-1 Program Management Plan. All changes reflected herein are designated
as **pending** until formal CCB disposition is recorded
. Upon CCB approval, this update shall be incorporated into the program baseline
within 30 calendar days per PRB Section 12.3, with propagation to
SEMP-0001, RVTM-0001,

TEMP-0001, IMS-0001, CBD-0002, and the Risk Register.

### 1.2 Background

and Change Context

The MAAP-1 program currently designates Samsung SDI as the sole primary battery pack supplier for
the Main Battery Assembly (AF-MAAP1-EP
DC 50Ah NiCd, two per
aircraft), the Emergency Battery Assembly (AF-MAAP1-EP-3002-00, 25Ah NiCd,
one per aircraft), and the Battery Temperature Management System (AF-MAAP1-EP-3005-00, three
per aircraft). The Risk Register entry RISK-SC-001 identifies single-source dependence on
Samsung SDI as an active **High-risk item (
Score: 12)** and explicitly names LG Energy Solution as the designated
secondary/contingency supplier currently undergoing qualification with a

target completion of Month 18.

The proposed change elevates
LGES from secondary/contingency status to primary supplier. This is not a zero
-to-new-supplier introduction — the program has an existing qualification
pathway for LGES. However, the elevation from secondary to primary carries materially different obligations
under FAA type certification, production approval, interface
control document (ICD) governance, and supply agreement terms. This PMP Update treats
the change accordingly: as a **moderate-to-high impact
, formally controlled configuration event** requiring structured CCB governance

, not an administrative procurement substitution.

### 1.3 Critical Preliminary

Finding — Chemistry Clarification Required

A material ambiguity exists that
must be resolved before CCB approval: the current BOM spec
ifies **NiCd (Nickel-Cadmium)** chemistry for all battery assembl
ies, while both Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution are primarily **lithium-ion/
lithium-polymer** manufacturers. The following two scenarios produce
radically different cost, schedule, and certification footprints and must be distinguished by

the CCB:

- **Scenario A — Like-for-Like NiCd Supplier Swap
:** If the change is a NiCd-to-NiCd substit
ution within the same chemistry family, the certification and redesign burden is substantially
lower. However, neither Samsung SDI nor LG Energy Solution is a primary NiCd aviation
battery manufacturer; the intended supplier relationship and applicable

product line must be confirmed.

- **Scenario B — Chemistry Transition to
Li-ion:** If the proposed change embeds a transition from NiCd to lithium-based
chemistry, this constitutes a design change of substantially greater scope,
requiring full DO-311A qualification, battery box thermal
containment redesign (AF-MAAP1-EP-3003-00 and
-3004-00), charger unit replacement (AF-MAAP1-EP-2004-00), and a
new FAA certification basis — adding an estimated
12–24 months to the affected certification thread and $
20M–$28M or more in additional program cost

.

**The CCB shall not approve this change until the chemistry question is formally resolved and
the applicable cost and schedule estimates are rebaselined against the confirmed
scenario.** All estimates in this PMP Update are presented for both

scenarios where they diverge materially.

### 1.4 Governing Authority

This
update is processed under PRB Section 12. Classification as a **Class
I change** is recommended given the confirmed or probable impact on
both the cost and schedule baseline (CBD-0002 and I
MS-0001). Class I changes require Program Manager approval with Milestone Decision Authority (MDA) notification.
The Chief Engineer serves as technical authority for ICD disposition

; the CCB Chair is the Program Manager.

---

## 2. Project

Scope

### 2.1 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change modifies the program's supplier configuration for the electrical power
storage subsystem. The directly affected Bill of Materials line items, and

the nature of their impact, are as follows:

| Part Number | Description | Qty

/AC | Impact Type | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00 |
Main Battery Assembly (28VDC, 50Ah NiCd) | 2 | Direct — supplier change

| High |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3002-00 | Emergency Battery Assembly (25Ah NiCd) | 1 | Direct — supplier change | High |
| AF-MAAP
1-EP-3005-00 | Battery Temperature Management | 3 | Direct — thermal interface revalidation | Medium

–High |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3003-00 | Battery Box Assembly — Main (
Ti Alloy, MAKE) | 2 | Indirect — form
/fit revalidation; redesign if Li-ion | Medium (

Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3004-00 | Battery Box Assembly — Emergency (Ti Alloy, MAKE) | 1

| Indirect — form/fit revalidation; redesign if Li-ion | Medium (Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-2004-00 | Battery
Charger Unit (Smart Charging System) | 2 | Indirect — charge profile

compatibility; replacement if Li-ion | Low (Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |

The
change also has secondary scope implications for the following

ICDs under PRB Section 5.3:

- **I
CD-002 (Electrical Power Interface — Mission
Bus, Load Shedding Priority):** Must be reviewed and
potentially amended to reflect any differences in LGES B
MS protocol, voltage/current output profile, or connector interface
. Controlled by the Chief Engineer, Electrical IPT.
- **ICD-003 (Core Avionics Bus / Mission System Data
Interface):** Must be reviewed if BMS data is integrated to the
vehicle health management or autonomous GNC system via the av

ionics bus.

The scope impact extends across all three MAAP-1 variants
— MAAP-1A, MAAP-1M, and MAAP-1C — given
that the electrical power architecture is designated as a common configuration boundary
under PRB Section 5.1. Any breach of the common
configuration boundary requires Chief Engineer and Program Executive Officer waiver.
### 2.2 Scope Boundaries — What This Change Does Not Cover

This PMP Update does not authorize, address, or pre-approve:

- Changes

to the propulsion system, rotor system, avionics architecture, or structural design
-
Modification of any KPP (Key Performance Parameter) th

resholds or KSA allocations
- Changes to the program's delivery schedule to
customers or contract milestone dates (such changes,

if required, are subject to separate contract modification under PRB Section 9.1)
- A
decision on chemistry transition (Scenario A vs. Scenario B) —
that decision is a prerequisite to CCB approval and must be made prior to or

simultaneously with change adjudication

### 2.3 Variant Coverage

All
three MAAP-1 variants share the primary wiring architecture and electrical power system. The ISR variant
(MAAP-1-ISR) drawing tree additionally identifies a 15
kWh battery assembly and dedicated Battery Management
System (MAAP-1-ISR-632000) and Emergency Power
Distribution Unit. Any LGES qualification effort must explicitly
address the ISR variant's battery configuration as a separate qualification article if its specifications

differ from the baseline MAAP-1A/M/C configuration.

### 2.4 Scope of This

PMP Update

This document updates the following PMP sections and baseline documents:

Baseline Document | Update Scope |

estones; long-lead procurement dependency rebaseline;

Monte Carlo re-run |

; new risk RISK-SC-001B; RISK-S-002 and RISK-C-003

elevated |
| PRB (Change Log, Appendix A) | CC

B action record upon approval |
| CBD-0002 | Cost baseline reassessment; N

RE and recurring cost delta |
|
RVTM-0001 | Battery-dependent requirements flagged for re

-verification |
| TEMP-0001 | Phase 5 environmental qualification scope updated

for LGES |
| Supply Chain Plan | LGES added as
Tier 1; Samsung SDI role updated; new

secondary supplier identification required |

### 3.3 Schedule

Impact by Scenario

**Scenario A — NiCd-to-NiCd

Supplier Swap (Like-for-Like):**

The qualification timeline is estimated at **
9–12 months** from contract award to FAI completion. Environmental
qualification adds a further **4–6 months** concurrent with FAA coordination
. Under a best-case, well-executed transition initiated
by Q3 2026, the critical path impact to airworthiness certification is estimated at **+2
to +4 months**, which falls within the existing 12-month certification reserve. The 01
Jul 2028 Long-Lead Procurement dependency remains protected with disciplined execution

. **Schedule risk: Medium.**

**Scenario B — Chemistry Transition to Li-ion:**
Full DO-311A qualification, battery box thermal containment redesign, charger unit replacement, and new certification basis establishment introduce
an estimated **12–24 months** of additional schedule activity. This exhausts
the 12-month certification reserve entirely and puts the Airworthiness Certification milestone (
Q4 2031 / 05 Jan 2032) at risk of slip to **Q4 2032–
Q2 2033**. IOC would slip from Q3 2034, triggering liquid
ated damages exposure. DTA-3 Avionics Integration Testing (Nov 2028 start
) cannot proceed with flight-representative batteries until chemistry-
change qualification is complete, potentially compressing or displ

acing avionics integration activities. **Schedule risk: High to Critical.**

### 3.4 Decision Deadline

To
protect the 01 Jul 2028 Long-Lead Procurement dependency and avoid placing battery supply on
the critical path for LRIP-1, the following hard decision deadlines apply

:

- **CCB Approval with Chemistry Resolution:** No later than **30

days from submission date**
- **LGES Contract Award:** No later than **Q2 2026**
-
**LGES FAI Complete:** No later than **Q1 2027** (Scenario A) or **Q3 2028** (Scenario B,

with concurrent DTA-3 schedule replanning)

Failure
to meet the Q2 2026 contract award gate will erode the existing 2
–8 weeks of positive float on the battery line items
in IMS-0001, potentially placing the battery system on the program

critical path.

### 3.5 IMS Actions Required

1. Insert all
milestones from Section 3.2 into IMS-0001 with assigned owners and predecessor

/successor logic.
2. Re-run the program Monte Carlo simulation (per
IMS Section 14.2) incorporating updated battery qualification
duration distributions for both Scenario A and Scenario B. Update P50
/P70/P90 outcomes for the Q4

2031 airworthiness milestone.
3. Update Section 14.3 (Recovery
Strategies / Backup Supplier Arrangements) of the I
MS to replace the current LGES-as-backup language with a

new named secondary supplier and associated qualification timeline.
4. Update Section 13.1 (External Dependencies
) to add a new row: "Battery Supplier Qualification (
LG Energy Solution) — Required by Q2 2027"

linked to the Long-Lead Procurement required date.

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification and

Approval Authority

This change is classified as a **Class I Change** under PRB Section 12.2. Classification drivers

include:

- Probable impact on the cost baseline (CBD-0002) through qualification
NRE, potential recurring unit cost delta, and schedule-

driven overhead
- Potential impact on the schedule baseline (IMS-0001) through certification re-work and

qualification activities
- Impact on ICD-002 (Electrical Power Interface), a

CCB-controlled document
- Impact across all three MAAP-1 variants via the common

electrical power architecture

**Approval Authority:** Program Manager (CCB Chair), with MDA notification required

.
**Technical Authority:** Chief Engineer (ICD disposition, RVTM re-opening, wa

iver authority for common configuration boundary impacts).
**Co-reviewing
Authorities:** Certification Manager (FAA/DoD compliance implications
); Supply Chain Director (procurement and contract actions); Program Cost Analyst (CBD

-0002 impact assessment).

### 4.3 Interim Control Measures (Pending

CCB Approval)

Prior to CCB approval, the following interim control measures are in effect:

1. **No de-commitment of
Samsung SDI:** The existing Samsung SDI supply agreement remains in full force. No termination action,
notice of intent, or procurement volume reduction shall be initiated

pending CCB decision.
2. **No LGES production purchase orders:**
LGES qualification activities may continue under existing secondary supplier engagement, but no production

purchase orders for LGES batteries as primary supply articles shall be placed without CCB approval.
3.

## 5. Cost Management

Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Program Financial Baseline Context

The MAAP-1 program is contracted
on a **Firm Fixed Price (FFP)** basis with a baseline gross margin of **$
,778M revenue. The Monte Carlo financial model shows only
**50% confidence** of achieving the 9.3% margin target, and
the program's risk reserve of **$74M is underfunded by
approximately $51M** relative to the P70 confidence level of
$125M. The current Supplier Risk reserve allocation
is **$15M**. The production cost per unit is already within
**8% of target**, leaving minimal buffer before trigg

ering the cost corrective action plan process under PRB Section 11.

In this
financial context, the proposed supplier change must be treated as a **significant financial event**, not a procurement
substitution. Every cost element must be identified, quantified, and formally allocated to an

approved reserve bucket before CCB approval is granted.

### 5.3 Recurring Unit Cost Impact

The battery
system currently represents **$202,000 per aircraft** in B
OM cost (EP-3001-00 and EP-3002-00 only)
or **$337,000 per aircraft** including temperature management (EP-3005-00). Across
192 production units, this represents **$38.8M –

$64.7M** in cumulative battery procurement.

LGES pricing relative
to Samsung SDI cannot be quantitatively determined from available program documentation. However, the following

parametric bounds apply:

| Pricing Scenario | Per-Aircraft Delta | Full

-Program Impact (192 units) | Margin Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| LGES 8% less favorable (Scenario A adverse

pts |
| Cost-neutral | $0 | $0 |

0 |
| LGES 8% more favorable (Scenario A best) | -$27,000 | -$5.2M favorable | +0.11 pts |
| Li
-ion cost premium 20% (Scenario B) | +$67,000 | +$12.9

M unfavorable | -0.27 pts |

**The break-even analysis for Scenario A requires
LGES to deliver a sustained per-unit saving of approximately $10
,000–$46,000 per aircraft** (depending on actual transition
NRE) **over the remaining production run** to

## 7. Procurement

/ Supply Chain Management Plan

### 7.1 Make/Buy Decision

— Affirmed as BUY

The CCB affirms
the **BUY** designation for all battery assemblies (
AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00, -3002-00, -3005-00). No conditions exist
under the program's Make/Buy Decision Framework that
would justify insourcing battery manufacturing at a production rate of
48 units per year. The question is exclusively which external
supplier(s) best serve the program. This determination stands regardless of chemistry scenario

.

### 7.2 Dual-Source Strategy Decision Required

The CCB must explicitly decide whether to
implement a **dual-source strategy** (LGES as primary, Samsung SDI retained as qualified secondary) or a **full cut
over** (LGES as primary, Samsung SDI decommitted, new secondary identified and qualified). The
program's supply chain resilience framework and the structure of RISK

-SC-001 were predicated on a dual-source posture. The recommended position is:

**Recommended
: Implement a role-swap dual-source arrangement —

LGES as primary, Samsung SDI as qualified secondary — not a full cutover.**

Rationale: A full cutover simply transfers
the single-source vulnerability to LGES. The dual-source arrangement retires the fundamental
risk that RISK-SC-001 was designed to address. The incremental cost of maintaining Samsung SDI qualification as
a secondary source is estimated at $300K–$600K in ongoing contract
and audit management — a modest premium for meaningful

supply chain resilience.

If the CCB approves a full cutover, a **new secondary supplier must be identified and a
qualification timeline established before the Samsung SDI relationship is formally de
committed**. Failure to do so re-creates the exact single-source condition RISK-SC-001 was

designed to retire.

### 7.3 Procurement Process — LGES Primary Supplier Establishment
The following procurement phases are required per the Supply Chain

Procurement Plan (Section 5.2) and must be executed in sequence:

**
Phase 1 — Market Research and Sourcing Strategy (Weeks 1–6)

:**

AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00 and -3002-00 specifications including chemistry, voltage, capacity, physical

envelope, BMS interface)
- Resolve the chemistry question (N

iCd vs. Li-ion) in coordination with the Chief Engineer
- Develop should-cost model for negoti

ation baseline
- Determine ITAR/EAR classification for battery
technology in the context of a Korean supplier on

a U.S. defense program; review applicable export control classifications
- Assess
LGES aerospace/defense program experience and financial commitment to

this market segment

**Phase 2 — RFQ and Proposal Evaluation

(Weeks 7–14):**

AS9100D quality requirements, capacity requirements (48 units/year FRP rate
), and program-specific terms (hell-or-high-water delivery, 24
-month EOL notification, tooling ownership, step
-in rights, source code escrow for BMS software

)
- Evaluate proposals against price, delivery reliability, quality system, capacity, financial

health, and geopolitical risk

- Do
not disclose pricing negotiation status to Samsung SDI until LGES contract

Negotiation and Contract Award (Weeks 15–20):**

etary BMS software

ity Plan During Transition

During the transition window (estimated Q3 2026 – Q4 2027

Both Samsung SDI and L

opolitical risk factors. The CCB must explicitly acknowledge this limitation in the change disposition record.

If

- **Ea
glePicher Technologies** (US) — established aerospace and defense battery

supplier
- **Concorde Battery** (US) — aerospace NiCd specialist

A U.S.-
based supplier would provide ITAR alignment, reduced tariff exposure, potential domestic
content compliance, and genuine geographic diversification — at potentially higher unit
cost. The CCB should task the Supply Chain Director with a comparative assessment

as part of the new secondary supplier identification effort.

LGES for its BMS IP before LGES is formally designated as primary supplier.** This
is a non-negotiable contractual protection. Cost of establishing the
escrow is an NRE item captured in the transition cost estimate (

Section 5.2).

urance Requirements

LGES must satisfy the following security requirements as a condition of Tier

1 supplier status on a defense aviation program:

NIST SP 800-171 for any CUI-handling scope
- **Hardware assurance testing

** per NIST 800-161 supply chain risk management framework
- **Counterf

eit parts prevention** per AS6081
- **Trusted supplier program

enrollment** per program security requirements

### 8.2

Safety-Critical Quality Controls

Battery assemblies feeding the
28VDC emergency bus are classified as **safety-critical items** under
the QMP hazard severity framework. The following quality controls are non-negotiable and apply regardless

of supplier identity:

- All battery failure modes with Severity (
S) = 9 or 10 in the FMEA require
mandatory action: design change, occurrence reduction, or detection

improvement. No exceptions.
- Emergency power loss is classified as **
Hazardous (Category II)** at minimum and
potentially **Catastrophic (Category I)** depending on flight phase. Required failure
probability: <10⁻⁷ per flight hour (

Cat II) to <10⁻⁹ per flight hour (Cat I).
- The BITE
detection threshold of ≥95% per Key Specifications
Section 9.2 must be re-verified against the LGES BMS architecture
. If LGES BMS fault detection coverage does not meet this threshold, a design change is required before

production release.
- Thermal runaway containment must be demonstrated for LGES cells under the
applicable abuse test protocol (DO-160G Section
15 for Li-ion; applicable NiCd test standard

for NiCd). This is a Government-witnessed test activity.

### 8.3 F

MEA and Safety Analysis Re-Work

The Safety Analysis and FMEA (W
-week duration in the TEMP) was
originally conducted against Samsung SDI cell chemistry and B

MS architecture. A supplier change requires the following safety analysis re-work:

1. **Design F
MEA Update:** Re-evaluate all battery-related failure modes against LGES cell chemistry,
BMS architecture, and pack construction. All S=9/10 failure modes must be re
-verified with new occurrence (O) and

detection (D) ratings.
2. **Process FMEA:** New Process FMEA for LGES manufacturing processes, with
particular attention to cell formation, module assembly, BMS programming, and pack-level

testing.
3. **Updated Fault Tree Analysis:** Re-verify the Electrical
Power System contribution to MTBCF (threshold: 5

,000 flight hours per Key Specifications Section 9.1) using LGES reliability data.
4. **
Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA) / System Safety Assessment
(SSA) Amendment:** If LGES battery architecture differs materially from Samsung SDI (
different cell chemistry, different BMS fault isolation capability), the FHA and SSA sections governing battery system
hazards must be formally amended and submitted to the DAL review

authority.

Target completion: Q3 2027, in time to

support FAA compliance package submission.

Re-Execution

environmental qualification sequence, all MIL-STD-810H qualification data
is supplier- and part-number-specific. Qualification data
accumulated under Samsung SDI is **not transferable to LGES**. A full

es (operating and storage)

and non-condensing)

C per MIL-STD-461G, MIL-STD-464D

, DO-160G

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Program:** MAAP-1 "AetherForge" | **Contracting Entity:** Eurus Systems
**Document Type:** Program Management Plan Update — Formal Change Action
**Change Reference:** CCB-MAAP1-SC-001 (Pending CCB Approval)
**Proposed Change:** Primary Battery Supplier Substitution — Samsung SDI → LG Energy Solution
**Contract Type:** Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
**Distribution:** Program Manager, Chief Engineer, Supply Chain Director, Certification Manager, Program VP, CFO
**Classification:** Program Sensitive — Internal Distribution Only
**Revision:** Draft for CCB Review

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update documents the programmatic, technical, financial, and supply chain implications of the proposed substitution of LG Energy Solution (LGES) as the primary battery pack supplier for the MAAP-1 "AetherForge" program, replacing Samsung SDI in that role. The update is prepared in accordance with the Program Requirements Baseline (PRB) Section 12 change control requirements and provides the Program Control Board (CCB) with a complete, cross-functional impact picture necessary to adjudicate this change.

This document supersedes or amends the corresponding sections of the standing MAAP-1 Program Management Plan. All changes reflected herein are designated as **pending** until formal CCB disposition is recorded. Upon CCB approval, this update shall be incorporated into the program baseline within 30 calendar days per PRB Section 12.3, with propagation to SEMP-0001, RVTM-0001, TEMP-0001, IMS-0001, CBD-0002, and the Risk Register.

### 1.2 Background and Change Context

The MAAP-1 program currently designates Samsung SDI as the sole primary battery pack supplier for the Main Battery Assembly (AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00, 28VDC 50Ah NiCd, two per aircraft), the Emergency Battery Assembly (AF-MAAP1-EP-3002-00, 25Ah NiCd, one per aircraft), and the Battery Temperature Management System (AF-MAAP1-EP-3005-00, three per aircraft). The Risk Register entry RISK-SC-001 identifies single-source dependence on Samsung SDI as an active **High-risk item (Score: 12)** and explicitly names LG Energy Solution as the designated secondary/contingency supplier currently undergoing qualification with a target completion of Month 18.

The proposed change elevates LGES from secondary/contingency status to primary supplier. This is not a zero-to-new-supplier introduction — the program has an existing qualification pathway for LGES. However, the elevation from secondary to primary carries materially different obligations under FAA type certification, production approval, interface control document (ICD) governance, and supply agreement terms. This PMP Update treats the change accordingly: as a **moderate-to-high impact, formally controlled configuration event** requiring structured CCB governance, not an administrative procurement substitution.

### 1.3 Critical Preliminary Finding — Chemistry Clarification Required

A material ambiguity exists that must be resolved before CCB approval: the current BOM specifies **NiCd (Nickel-Cadmium)** chemistry for all battery assemblies, while both Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution are primarily **lithium-ion/lithium-polymer** manufacturers. The following two scenarios produce radically different cost, schedule, and certification footprints and must be distinguished by the CCB:

- **Scenario A — Like-for-Like NiCd Supplier Swap:** If the change is a NiCd-to-NiCd substitution within the same chemistry family, the certification and redesign burden is substantially lower. However, neither Samsung SDI nor LG Energy Solution is a primary NiCd aviation battery manufacturer; the intended supplier relationship and applicable product line must be confirmed.

- **Scenario B — Chemistry Transition to Li-ion:** If the proposed change embeds a transition from NiCd to lithium-based chemistry, this constitutes a design change of substantially greater scope, requiring full DO-311A qualification, battery box thermal containment redesign (AF-MAAP1-EP-3003-00 and -3004-00), charger unit replacement (AF-MAAP1-EP-2004-00), and a new FAA certification basis — adding an estimated 12–24 months to the affected certification thread and $20M–$28M or more in additional program cost.

**The CCB shall not approve this change until the chemistry question is formally resolved and the applicable cost and schedule estimates are rebaselined against the confirmed scenario.** All estimates in this PMP Update are presented for both scenarios where they diverge materially.

### 1.4 Governing Authority

This update is processed under PRB Section 12. Classification as a **Class I change** is recommended given the confirmed or probable impact on both the cost and schedule baseline (CBD-0002 and IMS-0001). Class I changes require Program Manager approval with Milestone Decision Authority (MDA) notification. The Chief Engineer serves as technical authority for ICD disposition; the CCB Chair is the Program Manager.

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change modifies the program's supplier configuration for the electrical power storage subsystem. The directly affected Bill of Materials line items, and the nature of their impact, are as follows:

| Part Number | Description | Qty/AC | Impact Type | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00 | Main Battery Assembly (28VDC, 50Ah NiCd) | 2 | Direct — supplier change | High |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3002-00 | Emergency Battery Assembly (25Ah NiCd) | 1 | Direct — supplier change | High |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3005-00 | Battery Temperature Management | 3 | Direct — thermal interface revalidation | Medium–High |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3003-00 | Battery Box Assembly — Main (Ti Alloy, MAKE) | 2 | Indirect — form/fit revalidation; redesign if Li-ion | Medium (Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-3004-00 | Battery Box Assembly — Emergency (Ti Alloy, MAKE) | 1 | Indirect — form/fit revalidation; redesign if Li-ion | Medium (Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |
| AF-MAAP1-EP-2004-00 | Battery Charger Unit (Smart Charging System) | 2 | Indirect — charge profile compatibility; replacement if Li-ion | Low (Scenario A) / High (Scenario B) |

The change also has secondary scope implications for the following ICDs under PRB Section 5.3:

- **ICD-002 (Electrical Power Interface — Mission Bus, Load Shedding Priority):** Must be reviewed and potentially amended to reflect any differences in LGES BMS protocol, voltage/current output profile, or connector interface. Controlled by the Chief Engineer, Electrical IPT.
- **ICD-003 (Core Avionics Bus / Mission System Data Interface):** Must be reviewed if BMS data is integrated to the vehicle health management or autonomous GNC system via the avionics bus.

The scope impact extends across all three MAAP-1 variants — MAAP-1A, MAAP-1M, and MAAP-1C — given that the electrical power architecture is designated as a common configuration boundary under PRB Section 5.1. Any breach of the common configuration boundary requires Chief Engineer and Program Executive Officer waiver.

### 2.2 Scope Boundaries — What This Change Does Not Cover

This PMP Update does not authorize, address, or pre-approve:

- Changes to the propulsion system, rotor system, avionics architecture, or structural design
- Modification of any KPP (Key Performance Parameter) thresholds or KSA allocations
- Changes to the program's delivery schedule to customers or contract milestone dates (such changes, if required, are subject to separate contract modification under PRB Section 9.1)
- A decision on chemistry transition (Scenario A vs. Scenario B) — that decision is a prerequisite to CCB approval and must be made prior to or simultaneously with change adjudication

### 2.3 Variant Coverage

All three MAAP-1 variants share the primary wiring architecture and electrical power system. The ISR variant (MAAP-1-ISR) drawing tree additionally identifies a 15 kWh battery assembly and dedicated Battery Management System (MAAP-1-ISR-632000) and Emergency Power Distribution Unit. Any LGES qualification effort must explicitly address the ISR variant's battery configuration as a separate qualification article if its specifications differ from the baseline MAAP-1A/M/C configuration.

This document updates the following PMP sections and baseline documents:

| PRB (Change Log, Appendix A) | CCB action record upon approval |

### 3.3 Schedule Impact by Scenario

**Scenario A — NiCd-to-NiCd Supplier Swap (Like-for-Like):**

The qualification timeline is estimated at **9–12 months** from contract award to FAI completion. Environmental qualification adds a further **4–6 months** concurrent with FAA coordination. Under a best-case, well-executed transition initiated by Q3 2026, the critical path impact to airworthiness certification is estimated at **+2 to +4 months**, which falls within the existing 12-month certification reserve. The 01 Jul 2028 Long-Lead Procurement dependency remains protected with disciplined execution. **Schedule risk: Medium.**

**Scenario B — Chemistry Transition to Li-ion:**

Full DO-311A qualification, battery box thermal containment redesign, charger unit replacement, and new certification basis establishment introduce an estimated **12–24 months** of additional schedule activity. This exhausts the 12-month certification reserve entirely and puts the Airworthiness Certification milestone (Q4 2031 / 05 Jan 2032) at risk of slip to **Q4 2032–Q2 2033**. IOC would slip from Q3 2034, triggering liquidated damages exposure. DTA-3 Avionics Integration Testing (Nov 2028 start) cannot proceed with flight-representative batteries until chemistry-change qualification is complete, potentially compressing or displacing avionics integration activities. **Schedule risk: High to Critical.**

### 3.4 Decision Deadline

To protect the 01 Jul 2028 Long-Lead Procurement dependency and avoid placing battery supply on the critical path for LRIP-1, the following hard decision deadlines apply:

- **CCB Approval with Chemistry Resolution:** No later than **30 days from submission date**
- **LGES Contract Award:** No later than **Q2 2026**
- **LGES FAI Complete:** No later than **Q1 2027** (Scenario A) or **Q3 2028** (Scenario B, with concurrent DTA-3 schedule replanning)

Failure to meet the Q2 2026 contract award gate will erode the existing 2–8 weeks of positive float on the battery line items in IMS-0001, potentially placing the battery system on the program critical path.

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification and Approval Authority

This change is classified as a **Class I Change** under PRB Section 12.2. Classification drivers include:

- Probable impact on the cost baseline (CBD-0002) through qualification NRE, potential recurring unit cost delta, and schedule-driven overhead

**Technical Authority:** Chief Engineer (ICD disposition, RVTM re-opening, waiver authority for common configuration boundary impacts).
**Co-reviewing Authorities:** Certification Manager (FAA/DoD compliance implications); Supply Chain Director (procurement and contract actions); Program Cost Analyst (CBD-0002 impact assessment).

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Program Financial Baseline Context

The MAAP-1 program is contracted on a **Firm Fixed Price (FFP)** basis with a baseline gross margin of **$398M (9.1%)** on $4,778M revenue. The Monte Carlo financial model shows only **50% confidence** of achieving the 9.3% margin target, and the program's risk reserve of **$74M is underfunded by approximately $51M** relative to the P70 confidence level of $125M. The current Supplier Risk reserve allocation is **$15M**. The production cost per unit is already within **8% of target**, leaving minimal buffer before triggering the cost corrective action plan process under PRB Section 11.

In this financial context, the proposed supplier change must be treated as a **significant financial event**, not a procurement substitution. Every cost element must be identified, quantified, and formally allocated to an approved reserve bucket before CCB approval is granted.

### 5.2 One-Time Transition Cost Estimates

### 5.3 Recurring Unit Cost Impact

The battery system currently represents **$202,000 per aircraft** in BOM cost (EP-3001-00 and EP-3002-00 only) or **$337,000 per aircraft** including temperature management (EP-3005-00). Across 192 production units, this represents **$38.8M – $64.7M** in cumulative battery procurement.

LGES pricing relative to Samsung SDI cannot be quantitatively determined from available program documentation. However, the following parametric bounds apply:

| Pricing Scenario | Per-Aircraft Delta | Full-Program Impact (192 units) | Margin Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| LGES 8% less favorable (Scenario A adverse) | +$27,000 | +$5.2M unfavorable | -0.11 pts |
| Cost-neutral | $0 | $0 | 0 |
| LGES 8% more favorable (Scenario A best) | -$27,000 | -$5.2M favorable | +0.11 pts |
| Li-ion cost premium 20% (Scenario B) | +$67,000 | +$12.9M unfavorable | -0.27 pts |

**The break-even analysis for Scenario A requires LGES to deliver a sustained per-unit saving of approximately $10,000–$46,000 per aircraft** (depending on actual transition NRE) **over the remaining production run** to justify the change on cost grounds alone. This is achievable only with meaningful LGES price competitiveness, which must be substantiated by an RFQ and should-cost model before CCB approval.

**Recommendation:** The CCB shall not approve this change on cost grounds without a documented should-cost model demonstrating positive NPV. If the change is approved on supply chain resilience or strategic grounds, the cost case must still be transparently presented to the Program VP and CFO.

### 5.4 Reserve Adequacy Assessment

| Reserve Bucket | Current Allocation | Estimated Draw (Scenario A) | Estimated Draw (Scenario B) | Residual (Scenario A) | Residual (Scenario B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supplier Risk Reserve | $15.0M | $2.5M – $4.5M | $4.0M – $7.0M | $10.5M – $12.5M | $8.0M – $11.0M |

**Scenario A** draws are within manageable bounds, provided other concurrent reserve demands do not simultaneously materialize. Given the program's $51M reserve underfunding at P70 confidence, the Supplier Risk reserve should not be treated as a discretionary buffer — a formal reserve replenishment action must be initiated regardless of scenario.

**Scenario B** exhausts the Certification Reserve and, if schedule delay costs materialize, could drive total program cost impact to $75M–$111M, compressing gross margin from 9.1% to as low as **6.8% or below**, materially increasing the probability of entering the "Slow Burn" scenario already modeled at 3.1% margin.

### 5.5 FFP Contract Implications

Under the FFP contract structure, all qualification NRE costs are Eurus-funded unless a contract modification is negotiated with the customer. The following contracting strategies should be evaluated:

1. **Value Engineering Change Proposal (VECP):** If LGES transition produces documented recurring cost savings (e.g., unit price reduction, weight reduction enabling fuel savings, improved lifecycle cost), a VECP can be structured to share savings with the customer while recovering NRE costs.
2. **Customer-Funded Class I Modification:** If the battery change is attributable to a customer-directed requirement change (e.g., mandated energy density improvement, domestic content requirement), recovery of NRE through a contract modification is appropriate. Target 18–20% margin on the modification per program pricing strategy.
3. **Internal Funding:** If the change is Eurus-initiated for supply chain resilience with no customer-attributable driver, NRE must be self-funded. In this case, a formal reserve draw authorization is required from the Program VP and CFO before expenditure commitment.

### 5.6 Program Metric Impacts

The following program metrics tracked at each PMR under PRB Section 11 are directly affected:

- **Green Aircraft Commonality (% of recurring flyaway cost):** Currently at 80% target. Any LGES battery price increase relative to Samsung SDI degrades this metric and must be reported.
- **Cost Performance Index (CPI):** Target ≥0.95; red threshold <0.90. Unbudgeted qualification costs not captured in the WBS will pressure CPI if absorbed into existing work packages.
- **EAC Variance:** Threshold 5% overrun triggers corrective action plan. Scenario B NRE alone could breach this threshold.
- **Weight Growth Margin (C-6 curve):** If LGES battery pack mass differs from Samsung SDI baseline, the Weights IPT must conduct a mass properties re-check. Any mass growth must be reported against the C-6 curve at each PMR.

## 7. Procurement / Supply Chain Management Plan

### 7.1 Make/Buy Decision — Affirmed as BUY

The CCB affirms the **BUY** designation for all battery assemblies (AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00, -3002-00, -3005-00). No conditions exist under the program's Make/Buy Decision Framework that would justify insourcing battery manufacturing at a production rate of 48 units per year. The question is exclusively which external supplier(s) best serve the program. This determination stands regardless of chemistry scenario.

### 7.2 Dual-Source Strategy Decision Required

The CCB must explicitly decide whether to implement a **dual-source strategy** (LGES as primary, Samsung SDI retained as qualified secondary) or a **full cutover** (LGES as primary, Samsung SDI decommitted, new secondary identified and qualified). The program's supply chain resilience framework and the structure of RISK-SC-001 were predicated on a dual-source posture. The recommended position is:

**Recommended: Implement a role-swap dual-source arrangement — LGES as primary, Samsung SDI as qualified secondary — not a full cutover.**

Rationale: A full cutover simply transfers the single-source vulnerability to LGES. The dual-source arrangement retires the fundamental risk that RISK-SC-001 was designed to address. The incremental cost of maintaining Samsung SDI qualification as a secondary source is estimated at $300K–$600K in ongoing contract and audit management — a modest premium for meaningful supply chain resilience.

If the CCB approves a full cutover, a **new secondary supplier must be identified and a qualification timeline established before the Samsung SDI relationship is formally decommitted**. Failure to do so re-creates the exact single-source condition RISK-SC-001 was designed to retire.

### 7.3 Procurement Process — LGES Primary Supplier Establishment

The following procurement phases are required per the Supply Chain Procurement Plan (Section 5.2) and must be executed in sequence:

**Phase 1 — Market Research and Sourcing Strategy (Weeks 1–6):**
- Confirm LGES product offering against current battery specifications (AF-MAAP1-EP-3001-00 and -3002-00 specifications including chemistry, voltage, capacity, physical envelope, BMS interface)
- Resolve the chemistry question (NiCd vs. Li-ion) in coordination with the Chief Engineer
- Develop should-cost model for negotiation baseline
- Determine ITAR/EAR classification for battery technology in the context of a Korean supplier on a U.S. defense program; review applicable export control classifications
- Assess LGES aerospace/defense program experience and financial commitment to this market segment

**Phase 2 — RFQ and Proposal Evaluation (Weeks 7–14):**
- Issue detailed RFQ with full technical specification, AS9100D quality requirements, capacity requirements (48 units/year FRP rate), and program-specific terms (hell-or-high-water delivery, 24-month EOL notification, tooling ownership, step-in rights, source code escrow for BMS software)
- Evaluate proposals against price, delivery reliability, quality system, capacity, financial health, and geopolitical risk
- Conduct site audit of LGES aerospace manufacturing facility
- Do not disclose pricing negotiation status to Samsung SDI until LGES contract award; maintain Samsung SDI negotiating leverage

**Phase 3 — Negotiation and Contract Award (Weeks 15–20):**
- Negotiate FPIF contract structure for LRIP quantities (LGES is unproven on this program; FFP at LRIP volumes is premature)
- Include Economic Price Adjustment (EPA) clauses indexed to relevant battery material cost indices (lithium carbonate, cobalt, nickel as applicable to chemistry)
- Negotiate pre-priced options for FRP quantities (FFP target) with learning curve pricing commitments
- Include the following mandatory contractual provisions:
  - Tooling ownership by Eurus Systems (enables transfer to alternate supplier)
  - Step-in rights (Eurus may enter LGES facility to complete production if LGES defaults)
  - "Hell or High Water" delivery clause (LGES must deliver even if own supply chain is disrupted)
  - 24-month EOL/product discontinuation notification requirement
  - Source code escrow for any proprietary BMS software
  - Contractual delivery penalty clauses aligned to Eurus FFP delivery schedule obligations
  - 10-year production commitment or technology roadmap
  - Quarterly financial health reporting (D&B equivalent, minimum 3A2)

**Phase 4 — Samsung SDI Transition:**
- Do not terminate or formally reduce Samsung SDI engagement until LGES FAI is passed and first three production deliveries are accepted per quality requirements
- Formalize Samsung SDI standby supply agreement with defined reactivation timeline (target: 6-month reactivation from notice)
- Assess Samsung SDI contract for termination liability, minimum purchase commitments, and notice period requirements before any formal notice is issued. Quantify exit cost before committing to transition.
- Build LGES buffer stock (12 units minimum / 4-month supply) before drawing down Samsung SDI inventory
- Manage existing Samsung SDI-qualified units: units in buffer stock cannot be used interchangeably with LGES units without re-qualification. A qualified configuration control plan is required to prevent mixed-inventory errors. Samsung SDI stock not required for bridge coverage should be formally dispositioned (use, rework, or write-off) with cost captured against the Supplier Risk Reserve.

### 7.4 Supply Chain Continuity Plan During Transition

During the transition window (estimated Q3 2026 – Q4 2027), the program will carry elevated single-source risk as LGES qualification progresses. The following continuity measures are mandatory:

- Elevate battery inventory target to **60–90 days on hand** (above the standard 30–45 day target) during the transition window. This deviation is formally accepted and documented; it does not constitute a KPI failure.
- Samsung SDI supply agreement maintained at current production volume commitments until LGES FAI acceptance.
- Weekly supply chain status review for battery assemblies, reported to the Supply Chain Director, during the transition window.
- Supply chain risk score for the battery commodity is expected to temporarily rise above the standard yellow/red threshold (>35/100) during transition. This is formally accepted as a managed condition.

### 7.5 Geographic Concentration Risk — Explicit Acknowledgment

Both Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution are **South Korean companies**. This supplier change **does not achieve geographic supply chain diversification.** Both suppliers are subject to equivalent regional geopolitical risk factors. The CCB must explicitly acknowledge this limitation in the change disposition record.

If geographic diversification is a program objective (whether driven by customer requirements, DoD domestic content mandates, or program risk management policy), the following alternative suppliers should be evaluated as part of the new secondary supplier identification effort:

- **Saft** (France/US, TotalEnergies subsidiary) — established aerospace NiCd and Li-ion supplier
- **EaglePicher Technologies** (US) — established aerospace and defense battery supplier
- **Concorde Battery** (US) — aerospace NiCd specialist

A U.S.-based supplier would provide ITAR alignment, reduced tariff exposure, potential domestic content compliance, and genuine geographic diversification — at potentially higher unit cost. The CCB should task the Supply Chain Director with a comparative assessment as part of the new secondary supplier identification effort.

### 7.6 LGES Technology Escrow Agreement

The existing Samsung SDI BMS IP technology escrow agreement (established as a mitigation under RISK-SC-001) does not transfer to LGES. LGES uses a proprietary BMS architecture. **An equivalent technology escrow agreement must be negotiated with LGES for its BMS IP before LGES is formally designated as primary supplier.** This is a non-negotiable contractual protection. Cost of establishing the escrow is an NRE item captured in the transition cost estimate (Section 5.2).

### 7.7 Cybersecurity and Hardware Assurance Requirements

LGES must satisfy the following security requirements as a condition of Tier 1 supplier status on a defense aviation program:

- **Cybersecurity assessment** per NIST SP 800-171 for any CUI-handling scope
- **Hardware assurance testing** per NIST 800-161 supply chain risk management framework
- **Counterfeit parts prevention** per AS6081
- **Trusted supplier program enrollment** per program security requirements

These requirements are non-negotiable for a safety-critical electrical power component connected to the aircraft's 28VDC bus and emergency power architecture.

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Supplier Qualification Requirements

LGES must complete the following supplier onboarding sequence under the Quality Management Plan (QMP) before receiving any production purchase order. Source inspection is mandatory on the first three deliveries of each affected part number.

| Qualification Step | Standard | Target Completion | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supplier Quality System Audit | AS9100D (Rev D or later) | Q1 2026 | Quality Director |
| Financial Health Assessment | D&B ≥ 3A2 | Q1 2026 | Supply Chain Director |
| Capacity Assessment (48 units/year) | Program-specific | Q2 2026 | Supply Chain Director |
| Cybersecurity Assessment | NIST SP 800-171 | Q2 2026 | Program Security Officer |
| ITAR Registration Confirmation | 22 CFR Part 122 | Q2 2026 | Contracts |
| First Article Inspection — EP-3001-00 | AS9102 | Q1 2027 | Quality Director |
| First Article Inspection — EP-3002-00 | AS9102 | Q1 2027 | Quality Director |
| First Article Inspection — EP-3005-00 | AS9102 | Q1 2027 | Quality Director |
| Source Inspection — First 3 Deliveries (each PN) | Program QMP | Q2–Q3 2027 | Quality Director |
| Process FMEA Review (LGES manufacturing processes) | ARP4761 / AIAG | Q3 2027 | Chief Engineer / Quality Director |
| Special Process Qualification (if applicable) | Nadcap equivalent | Q3 2027 | Quality Director |
| Supplier Scorecard Established | Program QMP | Q4 2027 | Quality Director |

The QMP LRIP-1 FAI pass rate target on first submission is **80%**. For a new supplier on safety-critical items, plan conservatively for at least one FAI iteration cycle. The schedule accommodates one re-submission cycle without breaching the Long-Lead Procurement required date of 01 Jul 2028.

### 8.2 Safety-Critical Quality Controls

Battery assemblies feeding the 28VDC emergency bus are classified as **safety-critical items** under the QMP hazard severity framework. The following quality controls are non-negotiable and apply regardless of supplier identity:

- All battery failure modes with Severity (S) = 9 or 10 in the FMEA require mandatory action: design change, occurrence reduction, or detection improvement. No exceptions.
- Emergency power loss is classified as **Hazardous (Category II)** at minimum and potentially **Catastrophic (Category I)** depending on flight phase. Required failure probability: <10⁻⁷ per flight hour (Cat II) to <10⁻⁹ per flight hour (Cat I).
- The BITE detection threshold of ≥95% per Key Specifications Section 9.2 must be re-verified against the LGES BMS architecture. If LGES BMS fault detection coverage does not meet this threshold, a design change is required before production release.
- Thermal runaway containment must be demonstrated for LGES cells under the applicable abuse test protocol (DO-160G Section 15 for Li-ion; applicable NiCd test standard for NiCd). This is a Government-witnessed test activity.

### 8.3 FMEA and Safety Analysis Re-Work

The Safety Analysis and FMEA (WBS 3.9.5, 32-week duration in the TEMP) was originally conducted against Samsung SDI cell chemistry and BMS architecture. A supplier change requires the following safety analysis re-work:

1. **Design FMEA Update:** Re-evaluate all battery-related failure modes against LGES cell chemistry, BMS architecture, and pack construction. All S=9/10 failure modes must be re-verified with new occurrence (O) and detection (D) ratings.
2. **Process FMEA:** New Process FMEA for LGES manufacturing processes, with particular attention to cell formation, module assembly, BMS programming, and pack-level testing.
3. **Updated Fault Tree Analysis:** Re-verify the Electrical Power System contribution to MTBCF (threshold: 5,000 flight hours per Key Specifications Section 9.1) using LGES reliability data.
4. **Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA) / System Safety Assessment (SSA) Amendment:** If LGES battery architecture differs materially from Samsung SDI (different cell chemistry, different BMS fault isolation capability), the FHA and SSA sections governing battery system hazards must be formally amended and submitted to the DAL review authority.

Target completion: Q3 2027, in time to support FAA compliance package submission.

### 8.4 Environmental Qualification Re-Execution

Per the TEMP Phase 5 environmental qualification sequence, all MIL-STD-810H qualification data is supplier- and part-number-specific. Qualification data accumulated under Samsung SDI is **not transferable to LGES**. A full or partial re-qualification campaign is required for the LGES battery pack as a new article. The required test program includes:

- Temperature extremes (operating and storage)
- Altitude (14,000 ft PA operational as specified in Key Specifications)
- Humidity (condensing and non-condensing)
- Vibration and shock
- EMI/EMC per MIL-STD-461G, MIL-STD-464D, DO-160G

This environmental qualification sequence is a prerequisite for the Environmental Qualification Review (Program Month 60) and feeds directly into FAA and DoD certification authority acceptance. The TEMP Phase 5 exit criteria must be satisfied with LGES articles. The Quality Director and Test Director shall jointly develop a LGES-specific qualification test plan within 60 days of CCB approval.

### 8.5 RVTM Re-Opening Protocol

All requirements in RVTM-0001 that have been verified using Samsung SDI battery test data, or that are planned for verification using Samsung SDI articles, must be re-opened and re-assigned to LGES qualification activities. The Chief Engineer shall review each affected requirement to determine:

- Whether re-**Test (T)** is required (most battery-specific environmental and performance requirements)
- Whether re-**Analysis (A)** with engineering justification is acceptable (some interface and compatibility requirements)

Any requirement proposed for closure by Analysis where Test was originally planned must be reported at each PMR with written rationale, per PRB Section 11 requirements. The Chief Engineer has sole authority to approve Test-to-Analysis methodology changes for safety-critical requirements.

### 8.6 Quality Metrics During Transition

The following quality metric deviations are formally accepted as managed conditions during the transition period (Q3 2026 – Q4 2027):

- **FAI Pass Rate:** May fall below the 80% first-submission target for LGES (new supplier); recovery plan to be tracked at monthly PMR
- **Supplier DPPM:** LGES starts with no established performance history; tracked separately from Samsung SDI history until 6 months of delivery data is accumulated
- **Supply Chain Risk Score:** Expected to temporarily exceed the 35/100 red threshold during the transition window; weekly reporting to Supply Chain Director

These deviations are formally documented and do not trigger automatic escalation actions during the defined transition period. The Quality Director shall declare the transition period closed when LGES FAI is complete and the first three production deliveries pass incoming inspection.

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Consolidated Risk and Opportunity Assessment

The proposed substitution of LG Energy Solution as the primary battery supplier for MAAP-1 is a **strategically defensible but operationally complex action** that carries moderate-to-high program risk if not executed with rigorous discipline. The following consolidated assessment reflects the integrated findings across all analyzed program documents:

**Principal Benefits of the Change:**
- Retires the most visible element of RISK-SC-001 (Samsung SDI single-source concentration) if a dual-source posture is maintained
- Aligns the primary supplier role with a company that has been under active qualification since Month 18, reducing the zero-to-production gap
- Creates an opportunity to negotiate more competitive LGES pricing at full program volume, potentially recovering modest recurring unit cost savings
- Establishes LGES relationship depth that mitigates future supply disruption risk if Samsung SDI encounters financial distress

**Principal Risks of the Change:**
- Certification timeline slip of 2–6 months (Scenario A) to 12–24 months (Scenario B) against a certification campaign already rated Critical (Score 16) with finite reserve
- Qualification NRE costs of $2.85M–$8.8M (Scenario A) to $14.85M–$38.3M (Scenario B) against an underfunded risk reserve and a 9.1% baseline margin with no identified offset
- Transition-period single-source gap if Samsung SDI is de-committed prematurely, creating the exact supply vulnerability that RISK-SC-001 was designed to prevent
- No geographic supply chain diversification — both suppliers are South Korean, leaving geopolitical concentration risk unchanged
- RVTM verification closure regression across all battery-dependent requirements, with re-test obligations consuming test organization capacity during the flight test preparation period
- Chemistry question unresolved — the BOM specifies NiCd chemistry while both named suppliers are primarily Li-ion manufacturers, creating fundamental ambiguity in the scope and cost of the change

**Net Assessment:** The change is **executable and potentially beneficial** under Scenario A (NiCd-to-NiCd, disciplined execution, dual-source posture maintained). It becomes a **program-level financial event** under Scenario B (chemistry change, or if certification delays cascade to IOC slip and LD exposure). The decision to proceed must be informed by the chemistry resolution and a fully-funded risk mitigation plan.

### 10.2 Decision Framework for the CCB

The CCB should apply the following decision matrix in adjudicating this change:

| Condition | Recommended Decision |
|---|---|
| **Chemistry confirmed as NiCd-to-NiCd; LGES pricing ≥8% favorable vs. Samsung SDI; qualification can complete by Q1 2027** | **Approve** — proceed with dual-source implementation, funded via cost savings harvest |
| **Chemistry confirmed as NiCd-to-NiCd; cost-neutral or minor unfavorable; supply chain resilience is primary driver; Samsung SDI retained as secondary** | **Approve conditionally** — CCB accepts cost-neutral NRE as investment in supply chain resilience; full funding plan required |
| **Chemistry change to Li-ion embedded in the change** | **Escalate** — do not approve at CCB level. Escalate to Program VP and Chief Engineer as a separate design change requiring executive sign-off, customer notification, and full Scenario B cost/schedule rebaselining before any decision |
| **No material cost benefit; no material supply risk benefit demonstrable; Samsung SDI performing acceptably** | **Reject** — maintain Samsung SDI as primary with enhanced buffer stock and monitoring. Retain LGES as qualified secondary per existing Month 18 target |
| **Customer-directed requirement for supplier or chemistry change** | **Negotiate customer-funded Class I contract modification at 18–20% margin** — fully recover NRE plus schedule risk buffer before accepting the change |

### 10.3 Unconditional Prerequisites to CCB Approval

The following actions must be completed **before or simultaneously with** CCB approval. No CCB vote shall be taken until these items are resolved:

1. **Chemistry Resolution (CCB Gate 0):** The Chief Engineer must formally determine and document whether this change involves a NiCd-to-NiCd supplier swap or a chemistry transition. This determination must be accompanied by a high-level cost and schedule estimate for the applicable scenario. **Hard deadline: 30 days from CCB submission.**

2. **Should-Cost Model:** The Program Cost Analyst must complete a bottom-up should-cost comparison of LGES vs. Samsung SDI pricing at LRIP and FRP volumes, with learning curve projections. The NPV of recurring savings must be compared to the full transition NRE estimate. **Hard deadline: 30 days from CCB submission.**

3. **Samsung SDI Contract Review:** Supply Chain Director must identify and quantify any termination liability, minimum purchase commitments, and notice requirements under the existing Samsung SDI supply agreement before the CCB approves any action that would initiate de-commitment. **Hard deadline: 30 days from CCB submission.**

4. **Reserve Adequacy Confirmation:** Program Cost Analyst must confirm that the Supplier Risk Reserve ($15M), Technical Risk Reserve ($28M), and Certification Reserve ($2.7M) are collectively sufficient to fund the applicable scenario's NRE exposure, accounting for other concurrent reserve demands. If the reserve is inadequate, a funded replenishment plan must be presented to the Program VP and CFO simultaneously with CCB approval. **Hard deadline: concurrent with CCB vote.**

5. **Dual-Source Decision:** The CCB must explicitly decide whether to pursue a dual-source (role-swap) arrangement or a full cutover. This decision must be documented in the CCB disposition record.

### 10.5 Final Program Manager Guidance

This PMP Update documents a change that is manageable but not trivial, and whose ultimate cost and schedule impact will be determined primarily by **how early the decision is made, how clearly the scope is defined, and how rigorously the transition is executed**.

The single greatest risk to this program from this change is not the supplier substitution itself — it is the risk of an **undisciplined execution**: approving the change without resolving the chemistry question, proceeding without a funded mitigation plan, de-committing Samsung SDI before LGES qualification is complete, or treating this as an administrative procurement action rather than a formal configuration change requiring sustained management attention.

Executed with the discipline this PMP Update prescribes — resolved scope, funded reserves, dual-source posture maintained, early FAA engagement, and weekly management oversight — the change is executable within the program's existing schedule and cost framework under Scenario A. The program retains a defensible path to First Flight (Q2 2030), Airworthiness Certification (Q4 2031), and IOC (Q3 2034) with this change, provided all decision deadlines are met and no concurrent high-consequence risks materialize.

The Program Manager's recommendation to the CCB is: **Approve the change conditionally under Scenario A, subject to completion of all five unconditional prerequisites identified in Section 10.3. Reject any approval under Scenario B until a separate design change process with full executive authorization is completed.**
